title: Mapped types — transform every property
instruction:
A mapped type iterates over the keys of an existing type and produces a new type. This is how `Partial<T>`, `Required<T>`, and `Readonly<T>` are implemented.

Build your own Nullable mapped type.
successMessage: `[K in keyof T]` iterates over every key in `T`. The `?` modifier adds optionality. Mapped types are the engine behind all TypeScript utility types.

title: Conditional types — types that depend on conditions
instruction:
Conditional types choose between two types based on a condition: `T extends U ? TypeIfTrue : TypeIfFalse`. They're used in complex type utilities.

Create a conditional type that extracts array element types.
successMessage: `infer E` inside `T extends (infer E)[]` extracts the element type. TypeScript infers what `E` must be for the condition to hold. This is how `ReturnType<T>` and `Parameters<T>` work internally.

title: Template literal types — string pattern types
instruction:
Template literal types combine string literal types to produce new string types. They're used for typed event names, CSS properties, and API routes.

Build typed HTTP route patterns.
successMessage: `RouteKey` is the cross-product of all Methods × Versions × Resources — dozens of valid combinations. TypeScript generates all of them at compile time. Assigning `"PATCH /v1/users"` would be a type error.

title: infer keyword — extract types from generics
instruction:
The `infer` keyword declares a type variable within a conditional type. TypeScript infers what that variable must be to satisfy the condition.

Use infer to extract return types and parameter types.
successMessage: `infer R` in `T extends (...) => infer R` asks: "what does this function return?" TypeScript figures out `R`. This is the exact implementation of `ReturnType<T>` in TypeScript's standard library.

title: Recursive types — deeply nested structures
instruction:
TypeScript supports recursive type definitions. Types can reference themselves, enabling types for JSON, tree structures, and nested objects.

Define a recursive JSON type and a tree structure.
successMessage: `JsonValue` references itself — arrays of `JsonValue` and objects with `JsonValue` values. `TreeNode<T>` has `children: TreeNode<T>[]` — an array of the same type. TypeScript handles recursive types without infinite loops.

title: Distributive conditional types — apply to union members
instruction:
When a conditional type's check is on a naked type parameter, it distributes over union types — applying separately to each member.

Build a distributive conditional type.
expectedOutput: Type filtering works at compile time
successMessage: Distributive conditional types are applied to each union member: `ToArray<string | number>` = `ToArray<string> | ToArray<number>` = `string[] | number[]`. Wrapping in `[T]` prevents distribution.

title: Variadic tuple types — typed rest elements
instruction:
Variadic tuples let you spread types in tuple positions. This enables precise typing for functions that concatenate or split tuples.

Build a type-safe tuple concatenation function.
successMessage: Variadic tuples (`[...T, ...U]`) preserve the element types at each position. `combined` has type `[1, 2, 3, "x", "y"]` — TypeScript knows `combined[0]` is `1` and `combined[3]` is `"x"`.

title: Nominal types with branding — type-safe IDs
instruction:
TypeScript uses structural typing — two types with the same shape are interchangeable. Branding adds a phantom property to create nominally distinct types.

Create branded ID types for different entities.
successMessage: Branded types exist only in TypeScript's type system — no runtime overhead. `UserId` and `OrderId` are both `number` at runtime (they add fine), but TypeScript prevents swapping them. This catches bugs where IDs from different entities are mixed up.

title: Intersection types for mixins
instruction:
Intersection types (`A & B`) require a value to satisfy ALL constraints simultaneously. Combined with generic constraints, they enable mixin-style type composition.

Compose types for an auditable, serializable entity.
successMessage: Intersection types compose multiple type "facets" — `User` has all properties of `Entity`, `Identifiable`, `Timestamped`, `SoftDeletable`, and `UserFields`. Missing any property is a compile error.

title: Template literal types with Capitalize
instruction:
TypeScript includes `Capitalize`, `Uncapitalize`, `Uppercase`, `Lowercase` built-in type utilities for template literal types. They transform string literal types.

Build getter/setter method names automatically.
successMessage: `Capitalize<"name">` produces `"Name"`, so `Getter<"name">` is `"getName"`. The mapped type generates all getter/setter methods automatically from the property names. Add `"phone"` to the union and `getPhone`/`setPhone` appear.

title: Covariance and contravariance
instruction:
TypeScript is structurally typed with covariance for return types and contravariance for parameter types. Understanding this prevents subtle type errors with function types.

Explore function type compatibility.
successMessage: Return types are covariant: a function returning `Dog` satisfies `() => Animal` because `Dog` is a subtype of `Animal`. Parameter types are contravariant: a function accepting `Animal` is safer than one requiring `Dog` — it handles more cases.

title: Type-level programming — complex type utilities
instruction:
TypeScript's type system is Turing-complete. You can write complex type-level programs using conditional types, mapped types, and recursion.

Build a DeepReadonly utility type.
successMessage: `DeepReadonly<T>` recursively applies `readonly` to every nested property and array. Arrays become `ReadonlyArray<DeepReadonly<E>>` — no push, pop, or splice. Every property at every depth is immutable.
